currency.name: Norwegian Krone
currency.budgetNote: Recommended: 280 kr (about £20). Norway is expensive — possibly the most expensive country in Europe.

mapQuestions.question: Which continent is Norway on?
mapQuestions.answer: Europe — the far north-west, part of Scandinavia. 🌍

mapQuestions.question: What is the capital of Norway?
mapQuestions.answer: Oslo — a small capital city at the head of a long fjord.

mapQuestions.question: What is a fjord?
mapQuestions.answer: A long, narrow sea inlet between high cliffs, carved by glaciers during the last ice age.

mapQuestions.question: Which Norwegian city is famous as the gateway to the fjords?
mapQuestions.answer: Bergen, on the west coast — Norway's second city.

mapQuestions.question: What is the northernmost mainland town in Europe?
mapQuestions.answer: Hammerfest, on Norway's far north coast, inside the Arctic Circle.

foods.name: Salmon
foods.description: Norway is the world's biggest salmon producer. Smoked, cured, grilled — try it however it's served.

foods.name: Brunost
foods.description: Brown caramelised cheese, sliced thin with a special cheese plane onto bread or waffles. Sweet and savoury at the same time.

foods.name: Pølse
foods.description: Norwegian hot dog, wrapped in a soft potato pancake (lompe) instead of a bun. Petrol-station classic.

foods.name: Waffles
foods.description: Heart-shaped waffles eaten with brunost and jam. Available everywhere from cafés to ferry terminals.

foods.name: Reindeer stew
foods.description: Stewed reindeer meat with potatoes and lingonberry. A Sámi dish from the north.

foods.name: Kvikk Lunsj
foods.description: Norwegian chocolate bar — basically the same as a KitKat, but Norwegians swear it's different. Hiking essential.

scavengerItems.label: A mountain rising straight out of the sea
A car ferry crossing a fjord
Outdoor sports gear in a normal shop window
A wooden stave church (or a normal church with a tall steeple)
A house with a grass roof (still done in some country areas)
A real reindeer or moose road sign
A clear dark sky (perfect for Aurora hunting in winter)
A Viking ship replica or museum
A red rorbu (fisherman's hut) on stilts over water
Someone hiking in proper boots through a town

geoMatches.description: The capital. Vikings museums, the famous Munch "Scream" painting, and a brand-new opera house you can walk on the roof of.
Gateway to the fjords on the west coast. UNESCO-listed colourful wooden wharf, fresh fish market, and 240+ rainy days a year.
Norway's most famous landscape. Geirangerfjord, Nærøyfjord, and Sognefjord are the most famous. Each one is a flooded glacier-carved valley.
A string of mountainous islands in the far north. Red wooden fishing huts on stilts, white-sand beaches, and the Northern Lights in winter.
The biggest city inside the Arctic Circle. Best Northern Lights viewing in winter, midnight sun in summer.

senses.smell: Salty sea air, pine forest, smoked salmon, woodsmoke from log cabins, fresh-fallen snow…
senses.hear: Wind in fjord cliffs, fishing boat engines, gulls overhead, waterfalls roaring in spring melt…
senses.taste: Salty smoked fish, sweet brunost, dark rye bread, cold spring water, cloudberry jam…
senses.feel: Cold sea spray, soft moss between rocks, smooth grey driftwood, sharp Arctic wind…
senses.see: What is the most incredible thing right in front of you right now…

stories.question: Why does Norway have so many fjords?
stories.body: Fjords are long narrow inlets where the sea reaches deep inland between steep cliffs. They were carved by glaciers during the last ice age (12,000+ years ago). Huge sheets of ice slowly ground their way to the sea, gouging out U-shaped valleys 1,000+ metres deep. When the ice melted, the sea flooded the valleys. Norway has over 1,000 fjords — Sognefjord is 205 km long and 1,308 m deep. Together they give Norway's coastline an incredible length: if you stretched it straight it would wrap around the Earth nearly three times.

stories.question: Who were the Vikings — and what does that word mean?
stories.body: From about 800-1100 AD, sailors from Norway, Sweden, and Denmark set out across the seas in long wooden ships. They traded, settled, and (sometimes) raided. "Viking" originally meant "raiding" — to go "a-Viking" was the activity, not the people. They reached England, Ireland, France, Iceland, Greenland, and even North America (500 years before Columbus). The famous round Viking shields, longships, and runic carvings are all preserved in Norwegian museums. Norwegian DNA is still about 1% Viking by composition, and Norwegian uses many Old Norse words your English uses too: sky, leg, knife, husband, window, egg.
stories.thinkingQuestion: If you were a Viking captain, would you choose to trade or raid? Why?

stories.question: Why are there red houses on stilts everywhere?
stories.body: For 1,000 years, fishermen in northern Norway have sailed out into the Arctic in winter to catch cod. The cod come down from the Barents Sea to spawn — the world's biggest cod fishery. Fishermen needed shelters near the harbour, so they built rorbuer ("rowing-house") — small red wooden cabins on stilts over the water, so they could moor their boats right at the door. They're painted red because red paint was the cheapest (it was made from fish oil, ochre, and rye flour). Today many rorbuer are turned into cabins for tourists.

stories.question: Why is Norway so rich?
stories.body: In 1969, an oil rig in the Norwegian North Sea struck a massive field of oil and gas. Norway suddenly became one of the world's biggest petroleum exporters. Most countries spend that kind of windfall as fast as they earn it. Norway did something different — the government saved nearly all the oil money in a sovereign wealth fund that now holds about $1.6 TRILLION dollars (around $300,000 per Norwegian). The fund invests in companies all over the world; the country lives off the interest. Norway has free university, free healthcare, generous parental leave, and 5 weeks paid holiday — partly because of that fund.
stories.thinkingQuestion: If your family suddenly came into a huge amount of money, would you spend it now or save it for the future?

stories.question: What causes the Northern Lights?
stories.body: The Northern Lights (Aurora Borealis) happen when charged particles from the sun crash into the Earth's atmosphere and excite gas molecules — like a giant TV screen lighting up. The Earth's magnetic field channels them towards the polar regions, which is why you only see them at high latitudes. Different gases produce different colours: oxygen creates green and red, nitrogen creates blue and purple. The Sámi people of northern Norway have folk stories about them — some say they're spirits of the dead, some say it's rude to wave at them or you'll be carried off. Best viewing months: September to March.

convoQuestions.question: What's the best thing you've eaten in Norway so far?
Fjords are jaw-dropping. Which one have you liked most?
Could you live somewhere where it's dark for 2 months in winter and light for 2 months in summer?
Norway is the most expensive country we've been to. Was the price worth it for what we saw?
What's one Norwegian habit you'd like to bring home?
Norway has the world's biggest sovereign wealth fund — would you want your country to copy them?

wordSearch.hint: Norwegian foods, places and things to spot.

animals.name: Reindeer
animals.description: Both wild and herded by the Sámi. Often cross the road in the north.

animals.name: Moose
animals.description: The largest land animal in Norway — found in forests throughout the country.

animals.name: White-tailed sea eagle
animals.description: Huge wingspan — common along the coast, especially in Lofoten.

animals.name: Atlantic salmon
animals.description: Wild salmon leap up Norwegian rivers in summer to spawn. World-famous fishing.

animals.name: Harbour seal
animals.description: Hauling out on rocks along the fjords — sometimes pop their heads up next to boats.

animals.name: Polar bear
animals.description: Only on Svalbard archipelago in the Arctic. About 3,000 there — outnumber humans on those islands.

animals.name: Arctic fox
animals.description: Tiny white fox in the mountains and the far north. About 300 left in Norway.
